name: Senate Torture Report
description: The Senate Intelligence Committee Report on Torture, often referred to as the Senate Torture Report, is a 6,700-page report detailing the Central Intelligence Agency's (CIA) use of enhanced interrogation techniques on detainees in secret detention facilities (black sites) after the September 11 attacks. Released in a redacted 525-page executive summary in December 2014, the report concluded that the CIA's methods were more brutal than the agency had conveyed to policymakers and the public, and that they did not produce unique intelligence that could not have been obtained through conventional interrogation methods. Proponents of conspiracy theories often point to the report as evidence of a vast government cover-up, suggesting the redacted portions contain even more damning information about the extent of the torture program and the involvement of high-ranking officials. Some theorize the program was part of a larger, pre-planned effort to destabilize the Middle East or that it involved experimentation on detainees. Mainstream perspectives emphasize the factual findings of the released report, focusing on the ethical and legal implications of the CIA's actions. Critics of the report argue that it was politically motivated, biased against the CIA, and failed to adequately consider the context of the post-9/11 security environment. The redactions are explained by national security concerns and the need to protect sources and methods, although this fuels conspiracy theories about hidden truths.
subtitle: CIA's enhanced interrogation program controversy

claims.claim: The redacted portions of the report contain evidence of even more extreme torture methods.
claims.evidence: The sheer volume of the redacted material and the sensitivity surrounding its release.
claims.criticism: Redactions are standard practice in government reports to protect national security and confidential sources. The released summary already details extensive abuse.

claims.claim: The torture program was part of a deliberate plan to destabilize the Middle East.
claims.evidence: The timing of the program following 9/11 and the subsequent interventions in the region. The use of torture created instability.
claims.criticism: The program was a response to the perceived threat of terrorism following 9/11, not a pre-planned destabilization effort. The correlation does not equal causation.

claims.claim: Detainees were subjected to medical experimentation under the guise of interrogation.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal accounts and the lack of transparency surrounding the program's oversight. Some details of methods used are vague and could be interpreted as such.
claims.criticism: There is no credible evidence to support claims of medical experimentation. The report focuses on interrogation techniques, not medical procedures.

keyFigures.name: Dianne Feinstein
keyFigures.role: Chairman of the Senate Intelligence Committee during the report's creation.

keyFigures.name: John Brennan
keyFigures.role: CIA Director during the report's release, defended the CIA's actions.

keyFigures.name: Jose Rodriguez
keyFigures.role: CIA officer who authorized the destruction of interrogation tapes.

timeline.event: Senate Intelligence Committee begins investigation into the CIA's detention and interrogation program.
Senate Intelligence Committee approves the final report.
Executive summary of the Senate Torture Report is released to the public.
